title: The Psychology of Intimacy
subtitle: Connections, empathy, and conflict management
description: This checklist covers key aspects of building deep and sustainable relationships: from friendships to romantic. It is based on research into attachment, communication, and social intelligence.

items.title: Mirroring the Partner
items.source: John Gottman — The Seven Principles for Making Marriage Work
items.text: Bids for connection are small gestures of attention. Ignoring these bids destroys trust faster than loud arguments. Responding to a bid strengthens the emotional bank account.
items.tip: Notice when a loved one shows you something on their phone or comments on the weather — respond at least with an interjection.

items.title: Validation Instead of Advice
items.text: 90% of the time, a person shares a problem not for a solution, but for their emotions to be acknowledged as real. Unsolicited advice causes irritation and a feeling of being misunderstood.
items.tip: Instead of 'You need to do...' say 'It sounds like you're really tired of this. I understand why you're angry.'

items.title: Attachment Theory
items.text: Behavior in conflicts is often driven by attachment type: anxious clings, avoidant withdraws. Understanding your own and your partner's type lowers unrealistic expectations.
items.tip: If your partner wants to be alone after an argument, it's not necessarily the end of the relationship; it might be their way of recovering.

items.title: I-Statements
items.text: Phrases starting with 'You always...' or 'You never...' trigger defensive reactions in the listener. An I-statement describes the speaker's feelings and facts without blame.
items.tip: Replace 'You make me so mad when you're late' with 'I start to feel anxious and uncomfortable when I wait more than 15 minutes.'

items.title: The Physiology of Conflict
items.text: When the pulse exceeds 100 beats per minute, the prefrontal cortex shuts down. The person is physically unable to hear arguments. This is a state of 'flooding'. Continuing the argument at this point is pointless.
items.tip: Introduce a safe word and separate for 20-30 minutes to calm down.

items.title: Love Languages
items.text: We tend to show love in the way we want to receive it. But if your language is acts of service and your partner's is words of affirmation, they will feel unloved even if the floors are perfectly washed.
items.tip: Ask directly: 'What have I done recently that made you feel appreciated?'

items.title: Fundamental Attribution Error
items.text: We attribute our own mistakes to circumstances ('I was late because of traffic'), but others' to character ('He was late because he's irresponsible'). Recognizing this distortion reduces aggression.
items.tip: When annoyed with someone, ask: 'What circumstances might have caused them to do this if they are generally a good person?'

items.title: The Michelangelo Effect
items.text: In healthy relationships, partners don't just accept each other; they 'sculpt' the ideal version of each other, supporting their best aspirations. This is not criticism, but belief in potential.
items.tip: Support not only current achievements but also aspirations: 'I know you want to write a book. How's the plot coming?'

items.title: Personal Boundaries
items.text: Lack of boundaries leads to burnout and hidden aggression. Boundaries define where I end and the other begins. Saying 'no' is caring for the relationship in the long term.
items.tip: A polite refusal formula: 'I can't do that, but here's what I can do... (offer an alternative).'

items.title: Repairing Ruptures
items.text: Conflict is inevitable. What matters is not the absence of fights, but the ability to repair the connection after them. Successful reconciliation makes the bond stronger than before the conflict.
items.tip: Use humor, touch, or simple words of admission of fault to signal the end of the 'war'.

quiz.q: What is a 'bid for connection' in Gottman's theory?
Why do unsolicited suggestions often annoy people?
a: The person wants empathy, not a solution

quiz.q: What to do when pulse exceeds 100 bpm during an argument?
What is the fundamental attribution error?
a: We blame circumstances for ourselves and character for others

quiz.q: What is more important for long-term happiness: absence of fights or...
